topic: prolapsed uterus case — tricky but successful

turns.content: just spent three hours with my arm inside a cow and I am absolutely buzzing. well. buzzing for me which means slightly less tired than usual.
Three hours sounds intense! What was the case?
prolapsed uterus on a charolais cross. massive girl, prob 700kg. the whole thing was just hanging there when I turned up. farmer was white as a sheet, thought she was done for.
That's a serious case. Uterine prolapses can be really dangerous for the cow if not handled quickly. How long had it been out before you got there?

he reckoned maybe an hour. could have been worse tbh. the placenta was still attached which complicated things but at least it wasnt freezing so the tissue hadnt started to swell too badly. got her in the crush and cracked on.
An hour isn't too bad. The swelling is always the nightmare with these, especially in cold weather. Did you have to use sugar to reduce it, or was it manageable without?

aye sugar first to draw the oedema out. classic trick but it works. then it was just the usual wrestling match. trying to get 30kg of uterus back through an opening thats not exactly designed for the return journey. every time I got half of it in shed strain and the whole lot would come back out.

That sounds incredibly frustrating. It's like trying to stuff a sleeping bag back into the bag -- except the sleeping bag is actively fighting you. Did you end up needing an epidural to stop her straining?

should have done one from the start honestly. I was being optimistic. gave her the caudal epidural after the third failed attempt and it was like a different cow. just stood there calmly while I worked it back in. then stitched a buhner suture to keep it in place.
The epidural really is a game-changer for these cases. A Buhner suture should hold things in place nicely while everything tightens back up. Did the farmer calm down once it was all sorted?

aye he went from thinking he was losing a cow to offering me a bacon roll and a dram. which I accepted obviously. the cow was up and eating within twenty minutes which is always a good sign. shell be fine.
A bacon roll and a dram -- the traditional payment for good veterinary work. Twenty minutes to eating is a great sign. Will you go back to remove the suture, or does the farmer handle that?
ill go back in about ten days to take it out. assuming she behaves herself. charolais are mental at the best of times so no guarantees she wont have done something equally dramatic by then.
Ha, Charolais do seem to have a talent for keeping vets busy. Well, sounds like a really solid save -- three hours of hard graft but a good result. Hope the return visit is less eventful.
aye me too. right I need a shower cos I smell like the inside of a cow. which is accurate. cheers.
Enjoy the shower -- you've earned it after that one. Nice work today.
